short: Open a file in a context that guarantees closure.
what: Python's built-in mechanism for working with a file. The with block ensures the file is closed automatically when the block ends, even if an exception is raised.
tokens: Built-in function returning a file object.
The character encoding used to interpret the bytes.
A binding of the file object to the name f for the duration of the block.
requires: a path to a text file
important: the file is not tabular and pandas loaders do not apply
line-by-line iteration is desired
avoid: calling open without with in interactive work (forgetting to close)
reach: When the file is plain text without a tabular structure, or when one needs streaming access.
example:
with open("notes.txt", encoding="utf-8") as f:
    for line in f:
        print(line.strip())

short: Construct a file path in an OS-independent manner.
what: Builds a path object using the pathlib module. The / operator joins path components irrespective of the operating system's separator.
tokens: A class from pathlib representing a filesystem path.
The first component of the path.
important: the code may run on multiple operating systems
paths are built from several components
avoid: hard-coding paths with backslashes or forward slashes in string form
reach: Whenever a path is constructed; preferable to string concatenation in all cases.
example:
from pathlib import Path
p = Path("data") / "encounters.csv"
df = pd.read_csv(p)

short: Read a comma-separated file into a DataFrame.
what: Reads a delimited text file from disk and returns a pandas DataFrame in memory. The default delimiter is a comma and the default encoding is UTF-8.
tokens: The conventional alias for the pandas library after import pandas as pd.
A pandas function that parses a text file containing delimited records.
A path to the file. May be absolute or relative to the working directory of the notebook.
requires: a file path that resolves to a readable file
important: the delimiter is not a comma (set sep=';')
the file uses non-UTF-8 encoding (set encoding='latin-1' or 'cp1252')
date columns should be parsed at load time (parse_dates=[...])
avoid: loading a file larger than available memory without nrows= or chunksize=
ignoring encoding errors silently
reach: When the input is a delimited text file and the entire table is intended to be present in memory.
example:
df = pd.read_csv("data/encounters.csv",
                 encoding="utf-8",
                 sep=",",
                 parse_dates=["admission_date"])

short: Read an Excel sheet into a DataFrame.
what: Reads a single sheet of an Excel workbook into a DataFrame. Requires the auxiliary package openpyxl for .xlsx files.
tokens: Pandas function that parses an Excel workbook.
Which sheet to read. May also be an integer index or None to obtain a dictionary of all sheets.
requires: a workbook with at least one readable sheet
important: the workbook contains formulas (values are read, not formulas)
multiple sheets are required (use sheet_name=None)
avoid: relying on cell formatting (colour, font) as data — it is not preserved
reach: When the source data is an Excel workbook, particularly when the file already contains tabular structure rather than complex layout.

short: Read a JSON document into a DataFrame.
what: Parses a JSON file or JSON string into a DataFrame. The structure of the JSON determines how rows and columns are inferred.
tokens: The path or URL of the JSON resource.
requires: a JSON document whose structure is convertible to tabular form
important: the JSON is deeply nested (consider pd.json_normalize instead)
records are stored under a key (orient='records' or extracting first)
avoid: reading very large JSON streams without preprocessing
reach: When data is delivered by an API or stored in a JSON file with a tabular structure.

short: Read a Parquet file into a DataFrame.
what: Reads a columnar binary format optimised for analytical workloads. Considerably faster and smaller than CSV for the same data.
tokens: The path to the Parquet file or directory.
important: working with intermediate files between Python steps
data exceeds approximately 10⁶ rows
avoid: sharing data with non-technical recipients (Parquet is not human-readable)
reach: For intermediate storage between analysis steps, or when working with files generated by data engineering pipelines.

short: Return descriptive statistics for numeric columns.
what: Returns count, mean, standard deviation, minimum, the three quartiles, and maximum for each numeric column. With include='all', non-numeric columns are also summarised.
requires: a DataFrame containing at least one numeric column
important: one wishes to see the central tendency, spread, and range of every numeric variable in a single view
avoid: interpreting describe on categorical columns without include='all'
reach: For an initial impression of the distribution of every numeric variable.

short: Return the data type of each column.
what: Returns a Series mapping column names to their dtype. An attribute, not a method.
tokens: Attribute returning a Series of dtypes per column.
important: one suspects a column was loaded as object when a numeric type was expected
reach: To verify that types were inferred correctly, particularly after loading from CSV.

short: Count duplicate rows.
what: duplicated() returns a boolean Series marking rows that repeat a previous row; sum() counts the marked rows.
tokens: Returns True for rows that are exact repetitions of an earlier row.
important: the data is an export from another system that may have produced repeated rows
avoid: assuming uniqueness without checking duplicated(subset=[key]) for the key column
reach: Routinely after loading from an external system.

short: Return the first n rows.
what: Returns the first n rows of the DataFrame as a smaller DataFrame. The default is five.
tokens: The number of rows to return; optional.
important: one wishes to inspect the structure of the data without printing the entire table
avoid: relying on head to identify anomalies — the leading rows are not a random sample
reach: To verify that the loader produced the expected columns, and to read example values.

short: Print a concise summary of the DataFrame.
what: Prints, for each column, its name, the count of non-null values, and the dtype. Also reports total memory use. The output is printed, not returned.
tokens: A method that prints structural information.
important: one wishes to see, in a single view, types and missingness across all columns
avoid: expecting a return value to assign — the method prints rather than returns
reach: As the second check after loading, when one wishes a single view of types and non-null counts.

short: Count missing values per column.
what: Composes two methods: isna() returns a boolean DataFrame in which True marks missing cells; sum() then totals the True values along each column.
tokens: Returns a boolean DataFrame with True where the value is NaN.
Sums the boolean values along columns (treating True as 1).
important: one wishes to know, for every column, how many values are missing
precedes any decision about imputation or dropping
reach: Routinely after loading, to determine the prevalence of missingness in each column.

short: Return a random sample of n rows.
what: Returns a random sample of n rows. Unlike head, the sample is not biased toward the start of the file.
tokens: Method returning a random subset of rows.
important: the data is sorted or grouped at load time and head would not be representative
avoid: sampling without random_state when reproducibility is needed
reach: When inspecting a file whose order is not random, particularly large export files.

short: Return the dimensions of the DataFrame.
what: Returns a two-tuple containing the number of rows and the number of columns. An attribute, not a method — therefore no parentheses.
important: the first check after loading; confirms the loader produced the expected size
avoid: confusing with .size, which returns the total number of cells
reach: Immediately after loading, and after any operation that may change the number of rows.

short: Count occurrences of each unique value in a column.
what: Returns a Series whose index is the unique values of the column and whose values are the counts, sorted descending.
tokens: Selects the column named col from the DataFrame, returning a Series.
Method counting the frequency of each unique value.
requires: a column (Series), typically categorical or of low cardinality
important: one wishes to inspect the categories present in a column, and their balance
missing values should be counted explicitly (use dropna=False)
avoid: calling on a continuous numeric column with many distinct values (produces a long, unhelpful list)
reach: For any categorical or low-cardinality column, to inspect the categories present and their balance.

short: Cast a column to a specified type.
what: Returns a Series whose values have been converted to the requested type. Fails on values that cannot be converted (including NaN for integer types).
tokens: Method casting to a new dtype.
The target type. May also be float, str, 'category', or a numpy dtype.
requires: a column whose values are convertible to the target type
important: a column was loaded as object when a numeric type was expected
memory savings are desired (cast to 'category')
avoid: casting a column with NaN to int (use 'Int64' instead, which supports missing values)
reach: When a column's inferred type does not match its intended use.
example:
df["age"] = df["age"].astype("Int64")
df["ward"] = df["ward"].astype("category")

short: Constrain values to a specified range.
what: Returns a Series in which values below lower are set to lower and values above upper are set to upper.
tokens: Method bounding values to a range.
important: the domain has known physical bounds (age, percentages)
outliers are known to be input errors
avoid: clipping silently — values at the bound become indistinguishable, which may bias subsequent statistics
reach: When values outside a plausible range are best treated as the bound rather than dropped.
example: df["age"] = df["age"].clip(lower=0, upper=120)

short: Remove duplicate rows.
what: Returns a DataFrame in which rows whose values in the subset columns repeat earlier rows are removed. keep selects which occurrence to retain.
tokens: Restricts the duplicate check to specified columns.
Retains the last occurrence rather than the first.
important: the data are exported from a system whose updates produce successive rows for the same entity
avoid: applying without subset when only certain columns define identity
reach: After loading, when the source system may produce repeated records.

short: Remove rows that contain missing values.
what: Returns a DataFrame from which rows containing NaN in the specified columns have been removed. Without subset, any NaN in any column causes the row to be removed.
tokens: Method removing rows with missing values.
Restricts the check to specified columns; only NaN in these columns triggers removal.
requires: a DataFrame containing at least one NaN
important: the column listed in subset is essential to the analysis
the loss of rows is acceptable (typically below five per cent)
avoid: calling dropna() with no arguments on real data — it removes any row with any missing value, often discarding most of the table
reach: When a value in an essential column is missing and no defensible substitute exists.

short: Propagate the most recent known value forward.
what: Replaces each NaN with the most recent non-missing value preceding it. bfill propagates backward.
requires: data with a meaningful order (typically time)
important: the underlying state changes infrequently and the last observation is a reasonable proxy
avoid: filling across long gaps where the state is likely to have changed
reach: For sparse measurements where the most recent reading is the best available estimate.

short: Replace missing values with a specified substitute.
what: Returns a Series (or DataFrame) in which NaN is replaced by the value supplied. The substitute may be a scalar, a dictionary mapping columns to values, or another Series aligned on the index.
tokens: Selects the column to operate on.
The substitute. For numeric columns, frequently df['col'].median(); for categorical, a sentinel such as 'unknown'.
important: the missingness is plausibly random rather than informative
the column is numeric and a central tendency provides a sensible default
avoid: filling with zero unless zero is a meaningful default in the domain
filling more than approximately a third of a column without acknowledging the assumption
reach: When a missing value can be defensibly replaced rather than dropped or allowed to propagate.
example:
df["age"] = df["age"].fillna(df["age"].median())
df = df.fillna({"gender": "unknown", "age": df["age"].median()})

short: Estimate missing values by interpolation between known values.
what: Replaces NaN with values estimated from neighbouring known values. The default is linear interpolation.
requires: a numeric Series with known values on both sides of the missing cells
important: the data are ordered (typically time-indexed)
the underlying process is plausibly continuous
avoid: interpolating across long stretches of missing data
using on categorical columns
reach: For time series with isolated missing values within an otherwise continuous record.

short: Map specific values to replacements.
what: Returns a Series in which exact-match values are replaced by their counterparts in the mapping dictionary.
tokens: A mapping from old values to new values.
important: categorical encoding must be made consistent
sentinel codes (-99, 999) should be mapped to NaN
avoid: using replace for partial-string substitution — use str.replace
reach: When category labels must be standardised to a canonical form.
example:
import numpy as np
df["score"] = df["score"].replace({-99: np.nan})

short: Normalise string values.
what: The .str accessor exposes string methods that operate element-wise. .strip() removes leading/trailing whitespace; .lower() converts to lowercase.
tokens: Accessor for vectorised string operations on a Series of strings.
important: the same category appears in several spellings
whitespace from an export distorts grouping
avoid: chaining many string operations without intermediate inspection
reach: Before any operation that depends on string equality (grouping, joining, filtering by value).
example:
df["gender"] = (df["gender"]
                  .str.strip()
                  .str.lower()
                  .replace({"m": "male", "w": "female"}))

short: Parse a column of date strings into datetime objects.
what: Converts strings to datetime64. With errors='coerce', unparseable values become NaT (not-a-time).
requires: a column whose values represent dates
important: date arithmetic, sorting, or time-based aggregation is required
avoid: relying on auto-inference for ambiguous formats (provide format= explicitly)
reach: Whenever a column representing dates was loaded as text.
example:
df["admission_date"] = pd.to_datetime(
    df["admission_date"], format="%Y-%m-%d", errors="coerce")

short: Convert a column to numeric, coercing failures to NaN.
what: Converts strings to numbers. With errors='coerce', values that cannot be parsed become NaN rather than raising an exception.
tokens: Function converting to a numeric type.
Values that fail conversion are replaced by NaN.
requires: a column whose values are mostly numeric in string form
important: the column was loaded as string (object) due to stray non-numeric values
avoid: coercing silently in production data without auditing the resulting NaN count
reach: When numeric data have been polluted by stray text (e.g. a currency sign, a footnote marker).

short: Bin a continuous variable into named ranges.
what: Returns a categorical Series in which each value is assigned to the bin whose range contains it.
tokens: Names assigned to the resulting categories.
requires: bin edges that cover the range of values
important: a continuous variable must be summarised by category for plotting or grouping
avoid: forgetting that bins are right-inclusive by default
reach: When age, time, or any continuous variable should be discretised for analysis.
example:
df["age_group"] = pd.cut(df["age"],
                          bins=[0,18,65,120],
                          labels=["child","adult","senior"])

short: Filter rows by a boolean condition.
what: Returns a DataFrame containing only those rows for which the boolean expression evaluates to true. The expression is evaluated element-wise across the column.
tokens: A boolean Series — True where age exceeds 65.
Selects rows where the supplied mask is True.
important: combining multiple conditions: each must be parenthesised, and the operators are & (and) and | (or)
avoid: using Python's and / or instead of & / |
writing back into a filtered view (use .loc for assignment)
reach: For any row-level filter on a single or compound condition.

short: Filter rows by membership in a set.
what: isin(values) returns a boolean Series, true where the column's value appears in the supplied collection.
tokens: Returns True where the value is in the list.
requires: a column and a list (or set) of acceptable values
important: one wishes to keep rows matching any of several categories
avoid: using a generator expression — isin requires a concrete collection
reach: For a set-membership filter — preferable to chained | comparisons.

short: Create a new column by vectorised arithmetic.
what: Assigns a new column to the DataFrame, computed element-wise from existing columns. The arithmetic is applied to every row in a single operation.
requires: the columns referenced exist and contain compatible types
important: the computation is the same for every row
performance matters (vectorised arithmetic is markedly faster than a loop)
avoid: writing a Python loop to compute a column that could be expressed as vector arithmetic
reach: For any derived numeric column where the formula is the same for every row.

short: Return the n rows with the largest values in a column.
what: Returns the top n rows ordered by the specified column. Faster than a full sort followed by head.
tokens: Method returning top-n rows by a column.
important: only the extremes are of interest
avoid: calling on a categorical column
reach: For top-N selection without sorting the entire table.

short: Multi-way conditional assignment.
what: Returns an array containing the choice corresponding to the first true condition for each element. A default value is used where no condition is true.
tokens: A list of boolean arrays, evaluated in order.
A list of values corresponding to the conditions.
Value used where no condition is true.
requires: matched lists of conditions and choices
important: several mutually exclusive cases must be expressed
avoid: overlapping conditions where the order accidentally determines the result
reach: For multi-way conditional logic that would otherwise nest np.where.
example:
conds = [df["age"] < 18,
         df["age"] < 65,
         df["age"] >= 65]
labels = ["child", "adult", "senior"]
df["band"] = np.select(conds, labels, default="unknown")

short: Two-way conditional assignment.
what: Returns an array (or Series) containing a where cond is true and b where it is false. Vectorised, applies element by element.
tokens: A boolean array or Series (typically derived from a comparison).
Value (or array) used where cond is true.
Value (or array) used where cond is false.
requires: a boolean mask and two alternatives
important: the new value depends on a binary distinction (above/below a threshold, in/not in a list)
avoid: nesting np.where more than twice — use np.select for multi-way logic
reach: For a two-way conditional assignment.
example:
import numpy as np
df["band"] = np.where(df["age"] >= 65, "senior", "adult")

short: Filter rows using a string expression.
what: Returns a DataFrame filtered by the supplied expression, written as if it were ordinary Python syntax but evaluated within the DataFrame's column namespace.
tokens: Method filtering by a string expression.
requires: column names that are valid Python identifiers (no spaces)
important: the filter is long enough that a string expression is more readable than a boolean mask
avoid: interpolating untrusted user input into the query string
reach: For longer or more complex filters, where the readability of a string expression exceeds that of a chain of & and |.

short: Rename columns or index labels.
what: Returns a DataFrame with the specified columns renamed according to the mapping.
tokens: Mapping from current names to replacements.
requires: a DataFrame with the named columns
important: column names from an export are not consistent with conventions used elsewhere in the analysis
reach: To standardise column names across sources, particularly before a merge.

short: Sort the DataFrame by one or more columns.
what: Returns a DataFrame whose rows are ordered according to the values of the supplied column(s).
tokens: The column on which to sort.
requires: the column(s) supplied exist and are orderable
important: the table is to be presented to a reader who locates rows by magnitude
avoid: sorting the whole table when only the top-N is required (use nlargest)
reach: To prepare a table for display or for selection by rank.

short: Compute different statistics for different columns.
what: agg accepts a dictionary mapping each column to the statistic (or list of statistics) to compute. Returns a DataFrame of summaries.
tokens: Method applying named aggregations per column.
requires: a column to group by; columns and statistics for aggregation
important: several different statistics are required in a single summary
reach: When the per-group summary involves more than one column or more than one statistic.
example:
summary = df.groupby("ward").agg(
    avg_los = ("los_days", "mean"),
    total_cost = ("cost", "sum"),
    n_cases = ("patient_id", "nunique")
)

short: Apply an arbitrary function to each group.
what: Passes each group as a sub-DataFrame to the function and combines the returned objects. The most general — and slowest — groupby operation; reach for it only when the task is not expressible with agg, transform or filter, such as a per-group top-N.
tokens: Calls the function once per group and recombines the results.
Per group: the three rows with the largest stay.
important: the per-group operation is not a simple reduction or broadcast (e.g. top-N per group)
avoid: a plain reduction (use agg) or a group-aligned statistic (use transform) — both are far faster and clearer
reach: When the per-group computation cannot be expressed by agg/transform/filter — used sparingly.
example:
top3 = (df.groupby("ward")
          .apply(lambda g: g.nlargest(3, "los_days"))
          .reset_index(drop=True))

short: Number of non-missing values per group.
what: Counts, within each group, the rows whose value in the selected column is not NaN. This is the crucial distinction from size: count excludes missing values and is computed per column; size counts every row in the group regardless of missingness.
tokens: Partitions the rows by the distinct values of ward.
Selects the column whose non-missing entries are counted.
Counts non-missing values in that column per group.
requires: a column on which non-missing entries are meaningful
important: the number of valid observations per group is needed (e.g. a denominator)
missing values must be excluded from the tally
avoid: the total number of rows per group is wanted including missing ones (use size)
reach: When the count must reflect only valid observations per group, for instance as the denominator of a per-group rate.

short: Running 0-based index of each row within its group.
what: Returns a Series, the same length as the DataFrame, numbering the rows within each group 0, 1, 2, … in current order. Useful for sequencing repeated events per entity — for instance numbering a patient's successive encounters.
tokens: Numbers the rows within each group 0,1,2,… in order.
important: each repeated event per entity must be sequenced (visit number, attempt number)
avoid: the row order is arbitrary — the sequence is then meaningless
reach: When successive rows belonging to the same entity must be numbered, such as the n-th encounter of each patient.

short: Keep only the rows of groups satisfying a condition.
what: Returns the original rows, restricted to those belonging to groups for which the predicate is True. The predicate receives each group as a sub-DataFrame and must return a single boolean. Unlike agg, the result has the same columns as the input — it filters rows, it does not summarise.
tokens: Keeps or discards entire groups by a per-group test.
The test: keep groups with at least ten rows.
requires: a predicate returning one boolean per group
important: groups that are too small (or otherwise unqualified) should be removed before analysis
avoid: a per-row condition is intended — that is ordinary boolean indexing, not groupby.filter
reach: When whole groups must be excluded by a group-level criterion, such as dropping wards with too few cases.
example: big = df.groupby("ward").filter(lambda g: len(g) >= 10)

short: First n rows of each group.
what: Returns the first n rows from each group, in the DataFrame's current order. Sort the DataFrame meaningfully first (for example by date) so that "first" carries the intended meaning, such as the earliest encounters per ward.
tokens: Takes the first two rows of each group, in current order.
important: a sample of each group is wanted, or the earliest/first records per group after sorting
avoid: the row order is arbitrary — "first" is then undefined
reach: When the leading rows of every group are required, typically the earliest records per group after sorting by time.

short: Compute the mean of a column within each group.
what: Splits the DataFrame by the values in ward, takes the column los_days within each group, and computes its mean.
tokens: Partitions the DataFrame by unique values of ward.
Selects a single column within each partition.
requires: a column to group by and a numeric column to aggregate
important: a summary statistic is required for each level of a categorical variable
avoid: forgetting .reset_index() when a flat DataFrame is needed for downstream use
reach: For any per-group summary of a single statistic.

short: Group by more than one key.
what: Passing a list of columns groups by their combination: one group per observed tuple of values. The result has a MultiIndex whose levels are the grouping columns; reset_index() flattens it back to ordinary columns for further use.
tokens: Partitions by the combination of the listed columns.
The grouping keys; one group per (ward, gender) pair that occurs.
Aggregates the chosen column within each combined group.
requires: an aggregation appropriate to the selected column
important: the question concerns a cross-classification (per ward and per gender)
the result will be reshaped — follow with reset_index or unstack
avoid: grouping by many high-cardinality keys, which yields sparse, tiny groups
reach: When the summary must be broken down by a combination of categories rather than a single one.

short: The n-th row of each group by position.
what: Selects the row at the given positional index within each group (0 is the first, -1 the last). Order-dependent like head. Commonly used after sorting by time to pick the first or most recent record per entity.
tokens: Selects the first row (position 0) of each group; -1 would be the last.
important: exactly one positional record per group is wanted (the first or the last)
avoid: the row order is arbitrary
several rows per group are needed (use head)
reach: When a single positional record per group is required, such as each patient's first or latest encounter.

short: Count the number of rows in each group.
what: Returns a Series mapping each group to its number of rows.
tokens: Method returning row counts per group.
important: one wishes to know how many observations underlie each group's summary
avoid: confusing size with count: count ignores NaN per column; size counts all rows in the group
reach: To accompany any per-group summary with an explicit count.

short: Named aggregation: control the output column names.
what: The keyword form of agg maps each desired output column name to a (source column, function) pair. It produces flat, explicitly named columns, avoiding the awkward MultiIndex that the list form of agg creates, and is the recommended modern style.
tokens: Aggregates each group, naming the outputs explicitly.
Output column n = count of id per group.
requires: for each output: a source column and an aggregation function
important: several statistics of possibly different columns are needed with clear names
the result feeds a report or a further merge
avoid: the list form agg(['mean','sum']) when flat named columns are preferable
reach: When a per-group summary needs several explicitly named statistics, ready for reporting or joining.
example:
summary = df.groupby("ward").agg(
    avg_los=("los_days", "mean"),
    n=("id", "count"))

short: Produce a wide-format summary indexed by two variables.
what: Creates a table whose rows are the values of index, columns are the values of columns, and cells contain the chosen aggregation of values.
requires: a DataFrame with the named columns
important: the summary is to be presented to a reader rather than processed further
avoid: pivoting on a high-cardinality column (produces an unmanageably wide table)
reach: For a presentation-ready summary indexed by two categorical variables.
example:
pd.pivot_table(df, index="ward", columns="month",
               values="los_days", aggfunc="mean")

short: Broadcast a group-level statistic back to every row.
what: Returns a Series of the same length as the original DataFrame, in which each row carries the statistic computed for its group.
tokens: Computes the group mean and assigns it to every row of the group.
important: a deviation from the group mean (or other per-group reference) is required
avoid: confusing transform with agg: agg reduces; transform broadcasts
reach: When a per-row variable depends on a per-group statistic (e.g. deviation from ward mean).
example:
df["ward_avg_los"] = df.groupby("ward")["los_days"].transform("mean")
df["los_above_ward_avg"] = df["los_days"] - df["ward_avg_los"]

short: Use one DataFrame to fill in the gaps of another.
what: Cell by cell: where a has a value, that value is retained; where a is NaN, the corresponding value from b is used. The two DataFrames are aligned on index and columns.
tokens: Method using b as a fallback source for missing cells in a.
requires: two DataFrames describing the same entities, aligned on index
important: a is the primary source but contains gaps that b can supply
avoid: confusing with merge — combine_first operates cell-by-cell on aligned tables, not key-by-key
reach: When two sources describe the same entities and one is to be used as a fallback for the other.
example:
combined = primary.combine_first(fallback)
combined.isna().sum()   # compare before and after

short: Stack two DataFrames vertically or side by side.
what: Combines DataFrames along an axis. axis=0 stacks rows (default); axis=1 stacks columns. ignore_index=True renumbers the rows of the result.
requires: DataFrames whose columns align (for axis=0) or whose indices align (for axis=1)
important: the two sources represent the same kind of records (e.g. successive months)
avoid: concatenating without checking that the columns match
reach: For combining records of the same shape from different periods or sources.
example: full_year = pd.concat([q1, q2, q3, q4], ignore_index=True)

short: Join two DataFrames on a shared key.
what: Combines two DataFrames row-by-row by matching values in a key column. how determines which rows are retained when a key is missing on one side.
tokens: Method joining the calling DataFrame with another.
The column present in both tables, used as the join key.
Retains all rows of the calling DataFrame; rows of b without a match are dropped.
requires: two DataFrames sharing a key column
important: the cardinality of the key is asserted (validate='1:m') to catch unintended fan-out
an indicator column is added during debugging to audit the match outcome
avoid: merging on a column whose name differs between tables without left_on / right_on
merging with duplicate keys on both sides — the result grows multiplicatively
reach: Whenever information from two tables must be combined along a shared identifier.
example:
full = encounters.merge(patients, on="patient_id", how="left",
                        validate="m:1", indicator=True)
full["_merge"].value_counts()

short: Treat several 1D arrays as columns of a 2D result.
what: Combines 1D arrays into a 2D array, with each input becoming one column.
tokens: Function forming a 2D array from 1D columns.
important: one wishes to build a table column by column from separate vectors
reach: When several 1D arrays of equal length should become the columns of a 2D table.
example:
ages   = np.array([34, 58, 72])
costs  = np.array([450, 1200, 2300])
np.column_stack([ages, costs])   # shape (3, 2)

short: Concatenate NumPy arrays along an existing axis.
what: Joins arrays end-to-end along the specified axis. For 1D arrays, the result is the two arrays placed in sequence; for 2D, axis=0 stacks rows, axis=1 stacks columns.
tokens: Function joining arrays along an existing dimension.
requires: arrays whose shapes match on all axes other than the concatenation axis
important: combining batches of data with the same number of features
avoid: concatenating arrays of incompatible shape — the error message is sometimes terse
reach: For combining NumPy arrays where pandas would be unnecessary overhead.
example:
import numpy as np
np.concatenate([a, b])             # 1D: end to end
np.concatenate([A, B], axis=0)   # 2D: more rows
np.concatenate([A, B], axis=1)   # 2D: more columns

short: Stack arrays horizontally (side by side).
what: Places the inputs side by side. For 1D inputs, the result is end-to-end (equivalent to concatenate); for 2D inputs, the arrays are joined column-wise.
requires: arrays with the same number of rows (or 1D arrays)
important: one wishes to extend a 2D array with additional columns
reach: For horizontal stacking — additional columns onto a 2D array, or end-to-end for 1D.

short: Stack arrays vertically (as further rows).
what: Places the inputs one above the other. For 1D inputs, each becomes a row of the resulting 2D array.
requires: arrays with the same number of columns (or 1D arrays of the same length)
important: one wishes to produce a 2D result from 1D inputs
reach: For vertical stacking of NumPy arrays; works uniformly for 1D and 2D inputs.

short: Draw a bar chart.
what: Draws bars whose heights are the values y at positions x. For categorical x, the positions are the categories themselves.
tokens: Method drawing bars on the axes ax.
requires: an axes object and matching x and y arrays
important: the question concerns comparison of a single number across categories
avoid: using bars for continuous time series — prefer a line plot
reach: For comparison of a single quantity across a small number of categories.
example:
ax.bar(df["ward"], df["occupancy"])
ax.set_title("Bed occupancy by ward")

short: Draw a histogram.
what: Bins the values and draws a bar for each bin whose height is the count of values in the bin.
tokens: Number of bins (or explicit bin edges).
important: the question concerns the distribution of a single continuous variable
avoid: accepting the default bin count without inspecting the result
reach: For the distribution of a single continuous variable.
example:
ax.hist(df["los_days"], bins=30)
ax.set_xlabel("Length of stay (days)")

short: Draw a line plot.
what: Connects successive (x, y) points with line segments.
important: the question concerns evolution over an ordered variable, typically time
avoid: using line plots for unordered categories
reach: For trends over an ordered or continuous variable.
example:
ax.plot(monthly.index, monthly["admissions"])
ax.set_xlabel("Month"); ax.set_ylabel("Admissions")

short: Label the axes and the plot.
what: Three methods setting the title and axis labels. A plot intended for any external reader requires all three.
tokens: Sets the label of the x-axis.
Sets the label of the y-axis.
important: always, on any plot intended to be read by anyone other than the author at the moment of writing
avoid: omitting axis units — they are part of the label
reach: After every plotting call, before saving or showing.
example:
ax.set_title("Bed occupancy by ward — Q1 2026")
ax.set_xlabel("Ward")
ax.set_ylabel("Occupancy rate (%)")

short: Save the current figure to a file.
what: Writes the figure to disk in the format implied by the file extension. dpi controls raster resolution; bbox_inches='tight' trims excess whitespace.
requires: a figure produced by previous plotting calls
important: called before plt.show() — calling after may save an empty canvas
avoid: saving without bbox_inches='tight' for figures with rotated tick labels
reach: Whenever a figure is to leave the notebook.
example:
plt.tight_layout()
plt.savefig("figures/occupancy.png", dpi=200, bbox_inches="tight")
plt.show()

short: Create a figure and a set of axes.
what: Initialises a Matplotlib figure (the canvas) and one or more axes (the coordinate systems on which to draw). The figure is the object eventually saved; the axes carry the drawing commands.
tokens: Function returning a figure and axes.
Width and height of the figure in inches.
important: every plot intended for external use should begin with this call (object-oriented interface)
avoid: mixing plt.-style and ax.-style calls in the same plot
reach: As the first line of any plot beyond a quick exploratory view.
example:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(10, 6))

short: Write the DataFrame to a CSV file.
what: Writes the DataFrame as comma-separated text. index=False suppresses writing the row index as an additional column.
tokens: Omits the row index from the output.
important: recipient is a human or a tool that reads CSV
UTF-8 encoding suffices, or a specific encoding is given
avoid: writing the index when the default RangeIndex is uninformative
using comma as separator when the data themselves contain commas (use a different sep=)
reach: For data delivery to a human reader or to a downstream non-Python tool.

short: Write the DataFrame to an Excel sheet.
what: Writes the DataFrame to an Excel workbook. Requires openpyxl.
tokens: Name of the sheet to create.
important: recipient expects an Excel file
avoid: using Excel as an intermediate format between two Python steps (Parquet is faster and lossless)
reach: For deliverables intended to be opened in Excel.

short: Write the DataFrame to a Parquet file.
what: Saves the DataFrame in a columnar binary format that preserves types and is substantially faster to read than CSV.
important: the data are to be re-read by another Python step
types must be preserved exactly
avoid: choosing Parquet when the recipient cannot read it
reach: For intermediate storage in a Python pipeline.

short: Pairwise correlation matrix of numeric columns.
what: Returns a square DataFrame of pairwise Pearson correlation coefficients between every pair of numeric columns, each in the range −1 to 1. Pearson measures linear association only; method='spearman' gives a rank correlation robust to monotone non-linearity.
tokens: The DataFrame whose numeric columns are correlated.
Restricts the computation to numeric columns, avoiding errors on text.
requires: a DataFrame with at least two numeric columns
important: screening for linearly associated variables before modelling
a Spearman correlation is needed for monotone but non-linear relationships
avoid: inferring causation from a correlation
interpreting Pearson on clearly non-linear relationships
reach: When the strength of linear association between numeric variables must be screened, typically before regression or feature selection.

short: Pairwise covariance matrix of numeric columns.
what: Returns a square DataFrame of covariances between numeric columns. Covariance shares the sign of the correlation but its magnitude depends on the variables' units and scales, so it is rarely interpreted directly — it is an input to other procedures.
tokens: The DataFrame whose numeric columns are used.
requires: a DataFrame with at least two numeric columns
important: covariance is required as an input to a statistical method (PCA, portfolio variance)
avoid: interpreting covariance magnitude directly — standardise to a correlation instead
reach: When covariance is needed as an intermediate quantity for a downstream statistical computation.

short: Running (cumulative) sum.
what: Returns a Series of the same length in which each element is the sum of all values up to and including that position. Order-dependent: sort the data meaningfully (typically by date) before applying it.
tokens: Produces the running total at each position.
requires: a meaningful row order (usually sorted by time)
important: a cumulative total over time is required (running cost, cumulative admissions)
avoid: the data is unordered or the row order is arbitrary — the result is then meaningless
reach: When a running total along an ordered axis is required, such as cumulative cost over time.

short: Difference from the previous row.
what: Returns a Series of the difference between each element and the one before it; the first element is NaN because it has no predecessor. Order-dependent — sort by the time axis first. periods=n differences against the value n rows earlier.
tokens: Subtracts each value's predecessor; first result is NaN.
requires: the rows ordered by the relevant axis (usually time)
important: the period-on-period change (not the level) is the quantity of interest
avoid: the data is unordered
forgetting that the first value is NaN in any downstream aggregation
reach: When the change from one ordered observation to the next is required, such as daily change in admissions.

short: Index label of the maximum value.
what: Returns the index label (not the value, not the position) at which the column attains its maximum. On a DataFrame, df.idxmax() returns, per column, the index label of that column's maximum. Distinct from max (the value) and argmax (the integer position).
tokens: Returns the index label where the maximum occurs.
requires: a Series with at least one non-missing value
important: the identity of the row achieving the maximum is wanted, not the maximum itself
the index has been set to a meaningful key
avoid: the maximum value itself is needed (use max)
the positional location is needed (use argmax)
all values are NaN (raises)
reach: When the question is which row attains the maximum — for example which ward has the longest mean stay.

short: Index label of the minimum value.
what: Returns the index label at which the column attains its minimum — the counterpart of idxmax. Returns the label, not the value (min) and not the integer position (argmin).
tokens: Returns the index label where the minimum occurs.
requires: a Series with at least one non-missing value
important: the identity of the row achieving the minimum is wanted
avoid: the minimum value itself is needed (use min)
all values are NaN (raises)
reach: When the question is which row attains the minimum, such as the least profitable DRG.

short: Tailedness of a distribution (excess kurtosis).
what: Returns the excess kurtosis (Fisher definition): zero for a normal distribution, positive for heavier tails and a sharper peak, negative for lighter tails. Indicates the propensity for extreme values relative to the normal.
tokens: Returns the excess kurtosis (0 for a normal distribution).
requires: a numeric Series with at least four values
important: assessing the prevalence of extreme values before relying on methods that assume normality
avoid: interpreting kurtosis on a small sample, where it is highly variable
reach: When the heaviness of a distribution's tails must be assessed, for instance before applying a method that assumes normality.

short: Arithmetic mean of a numeric Series.
what: Returns the sum of the values divided by their count. NaN is skipped by default, so the mean is taken over the present values only. Sensitive to outliers: a single extreme value shifts it noticeably.
tokens: Selects the numeric column to summarise.
Reduces the Series to a single number — the average.
important: the distribution is roughly symmetric, so the mean represents a typical value
the mean of a boolean Series is required — it is the proportion of True
avoid: the distribution is skewed or has outliers (prefer median)
the column is categorical or an identifier
reach: When a single representative value of a numeric column is required and the distribution is not strongly skewed.

short: Middle value of a numeric Series.
what: Returns the value separating the higher half from the lower half of the sorted column. Robust to outliers and skew: unlike the mean, extreme values do not pull it.
tokens: Reduces to the central value of the sorted data.
important: the distribution is skewed or contains outliers
a robust measure of central tendency is required for reporting
avoid: a value that can be arithmetically combined downstream is needed and the data is symmetric (the mean is then conventional)
reach: When a typical value is required and the column is skewed or contains outliers — the default choice for length-of-stay, cost, or income style data.

short: Most frequent value(s) of a Series.
what: Returns a Series of the value(s) that occur most often. The result may contain more than one entry when several values tie for the highest frequency, which is why a Series rather than a scalar is returned.
tokens: Selects the column, numeric or categorical.
Returns the most frequent value(s) as a Series.
requires: a Series with at least one non-missing value
important: the column is categorical and the most common category is of interest
a single value is required — take .mode().iloc[0], acknowledging ties
avoid: a continuous numeric column where no value repeats meaningfully
reach: When the most common category or value is the quantity of interest, typically for categorical data.

short: Number of distinct values.
what: Returns the count of distinct non-missing values in the column. NaN is excluded by default; pass dropna=False to count it as one further value. Used to gauge cardinality before grouping, pivoting, or treating a column as categorical.
tokens: Counts how many distinct values it contains.
important: the cardinality of a key must be checked before a pivot or a wide reshape
deciding whether a column is effectively categorical
avoid: confusing it with count (number of non-missing rows) — nunique counts distinct values
reach: When the number of distinct categories or keys must be known, typically before grouping or reshaping.

short: Fractional change from the previous row.
what: Returns the fractional change between each element and the previous one: (xₜ − xₜ₋₁) / xₜ₋₁. The first element is NaN. Multiply by 100 for a percentage. Order-dependent and undefined where the previous value is zero.
tokens: Computes the fractional change against the previous row.
requires: a numeric Series ordered by the relevant axis
important: a growth rate rather than an absolute change is required
avoid: the previous value can be zero (division yields inf or NaN)
the data is unordered
reach: When a period-on-period growth rate is required, such as month-on-month change in cost.

short: Value(s) at the given quantile position(s).
what: Returns the value below which the given fraction of the data falls. A scalar argument returns one value; a list returns a Series indexed by the requested quantiles. The interquartile range is quantile(0.75) − quantile(0.25).
tokens: Computes the value at the requested quantile(s).
The quantile positions; here the three quartiles.
requires: quantile arguments in the range 0 to 1
important: a robust description of spread is required (the IQR)
thresholds such as the top decile must be located
avoid: a very small sample, where quantile estimates are unstable
reach: When robust spread (the IQR) or a distributional threshold such as a percentile cut-off is required.
example:
q1, q3 = df["cost_eur"].quantile([0.25, 0.75])
iqr = q3 - q1

short: Ordinal rank of each value.
what: Returns a Series assigning each value its rank within the column. method controls tie handling: 'average' (default) averages tied ranks, 'min' assigns the lowest, 'dense' leaves no gaps after ties. ascending=False ranks the largest value first.
tokens: Selects the numeric column to rank.
Assigns an ordinal rank to each value.
Tie policy: equal values share a rank and the next rank is not skipped.
requires: an explicit tie policy via method when ties are likely
important: a ranking (league table) is required rather than the raw values
ties must be handled by a stated rule
avoid: leaving the tie policy implicit when ties matter to the result
reach: When values must be turned into a ranking, for instance to build a league table of wards by cost.

short: Asymmetry of a distribution.
what: Returns the skewness coefficient. Approximately zero indicates a symmetric distribution; positive indicates a long right tail (mean above median); negative a long left tail. A useful numeric check of whether the mean or the median is the better summary.
requires: a numeric Series with at least three values
important: deciding between mean and median as the central summary
checking a modelling assumption of symmetry
avoid: interpreting skew on a very small sample
reach: When the symmetry of a distribution must be quantified, often to justify choosing the median over the mean.

short: Sample standard deviation of a Series.
what: Returns the spread of the values about their mean, in the same unit as the data. pandas uses the sample standard deviation (divisor n−1) by default; pass ddof=0 for the population value.
tokens: Reduces to the sample standard deviation (ddof=1).
requires: a numeric Series with at least two values
important: the dispersion of a roughly symmetric distribution is required
results are compared with the mean (same unit)
avoid: the distribution is heavily skewed (the IQR via quantile is more interpretable)
comparing spread across columns on different scales without standardising
reach: When the variability of a numeric column must be quantified alongside its mean.

short: The distinct values themselves, as an array.
what: Returns a NumPy array of the distinct values in order of first appearance (not sorted). Use it to inspect which categories actually occur — for example to discover inconsistent spellings before normalising.
tokens: Returns the distinct values as an array, in order of appearance.
important: the actual set of categories must be inspected (e.g. to find inconsistent labels)
avoid: only the number of distinct values is needed (use nunique)
a sorted, counted view is wanted (use value_counts)
reach: When the concrete set of values present in a column must be examined, often during cleaning.

short: Relative frequency of each category.
what: Returns a Series whose index is the distinct values and whose values are their proportions of the total, sorted descending. Equivalent to value_counts() divided by the count, but computed in one step. NaN is excluded unless dropna=False.
tokens: Selects the column whose category proportions are wanted.
Counts occurrences of each distinct value.
Returns proportions (summing to 1) rather than raw counts.
requires: a Series, typically categorical or low-cardinality
important: the share of each category is more informative than the raw count
class balance must be assessed before modelling
avoid: a continuous numeric column with many distinct values (the result is long and uninformative)
reach: When the proportion of each category — rather than its absolute count — answers the question, such as assessing class balance.

short: Sample variance of a Series.
what: Returns the mean squared deviation from the mean, the square of the standard deviation. Its unit is the square of the data's unit, which makes the standard deviation usually preferable for reporting.
requires: a numeric Series with at least two values
important: variance is required directly, for example in a statistical formula or an ANOVA-style decomposition
avoid: reporting spread to a reader — the standard deviation is in the data's own unit and is more interpretable
reach: When variance is needed as an input to a further statistical computation rather than for direct interpretation.
